paragraphs.summary: This supervisory statement (SS) is relevant to non-EEA banks and PRA-designated investment firms in respect of their operations in the United Kingdom through branches known as ‘third country branches
This statement replaces the guidance material previously included in chapters 4 – 9 and 21 of the Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls (SYSC) section of the Handbook. It does not seek
The statement should be read in conjunction with the PRA Fundamental Rules and with:

All PRA-authorised firms are required to meet the statutory PRA Threshold Conditions on a continuing basis. For branches in the United Kingdom, the PRA’s authorisation applies to the whole firm theref

The PRA expects that the arrangements, processes and mechanisms implemented by a third country branch should be comprehensive and proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inheren

The PRA expects third country branches to have robust governance arrangements, which include a clear organisational structure with well defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility. All

A third country branch is also expected to produce and maintain a Management Responsibilities Map, which is a single, up-to-date document setting out the branch’s management and governance arrangement
Firms should be aware of the expectations set out in SS34/15 ‘Guidelines for completing regulatory reports’ i
The PRA expects that, taking into account the nature, scale and complexity of the business of the third country branch, and the nature and range of the financial services activities undertaken in the
The PRA expects a third country branch to take reasonable steps to ensure continuity and regularity in the performance of its regulated activities.
A third country branch should establish, implement and maintain an adequate business continuity policy aimed at ensuring, in the case of an interruption to its systems and procedures, that any losses
The matters dealt with in a firm’s business continuity policy should include:

A third country branch should monitor and, on a regular basis, evaluate the adequacy and effectiveness of its systems, internal control mechanisms and arrangements established in accordance with Chapt

paragraphs.summary: The persons referred to in Chapter 3 of the Internal Governance of Third Country Branches Part of the PRA R

The PRA expects a firm as a whole to ensure that its management is undertaken by at least two persons who satisfy the criteria in Article 91 of CRD IV.10
The PRA expects a third country branch to ensure that:

In the PRA’s view, the supervisory function does not include a general meeting of the shareholders of the firm, or equivalent bodies, but could involve, for example, a separate supervisory body within

sections.chapter_title: Employees, agents and other relevant persons

paragraphs.summary: In the PRA’s view, a third country branch’s systems and controls should enable it to satisfy itself of the suitability of anyone who acts for it. This includes assessing an individual’s honesty and co

Any assessment of an individual’s suitability should take into account the level of responsibility that the individual will assume within the third country branch. The nature of the assessment will ge
The Certification Regime provides a framework for third country branches to assess and certify the fitness and propriety of certain employees on appointment and at least annually thereafter.

In the PRA’s view a third country branch should ensure that the performance of multiple functions by its relevant persons does not and is not likely to prevent those persons from discharging any parti

A third country branch should normally ensure that no single individual has unrestricted authority to do all of the following:
